data:
Search results for "PLY yacc parser shift reduce conflict precedence example":

Title: Incorrect shift-reduce conflict resolution with precedence specifier
URL: https://github.com/dabeaz/ply/issues/101
Content: ## Use saved searches to filter your results more quickly. While learning to use PLY and experimenting with it I've ran into strange issue. However, the resolution path taken by PLY seems to be incorrect: PLY chooses to reduce rule instead of shifting next token, although token is of higher priority than the rule used for reduction. `if_stmt : IF stmt ELSE stmt FI. Token ELSE has higher precedence than IFX:. As such, I expect rule `if_stmt -> IF stmt` to have less priority than token ELSE, so shift-reduce conflict should be resolved by shifting (per documentation):. `If the current token has higher precedence than the rule on the stack, it is shifted.`. However, when shift-reduce conflict arises (state 11 in parser.out) PLY chooses to reduce using production `if_stmt -> IF stmt` instead of shifting ELSE token:. ELSE reduce using rule 7 (if_stmt -> IF stmt .). | IF stmt ELSE stmt FI`. ### Footer navigation.

Title: [PDF] yacc, LR parsing
URL: https://web.engr.oregonstate.edu/~huanlian/teaching/compilers/cs480/3-yacc-LR.pdf
Content: тАв Precedence of a rule = that of its last terminal тАУ See bison manual for ways to override this default тАв Context-dependent precedence (Section 5.4, pp 70) тАв Resolve shift/reduce conflict with a shift if: тАУ no precedence declared for either rule or terminal тАУ input terminal has higher precedence than the rule тАУ the precedences are the same and right associative ECS 142 Lectures 7-8 56 Using Precedence to Solve S/R Conflicts тАв Back to our example: [E тЖТ E * тАв E, +] [E тЖТE * EтАв, +] [E тЖТ тАв E + E, +] тЗТE [E тЖТE тАв + E, +] тАж тАж тАв Will choose reduce because precedence of rule E тЖТ E * E is higher than of terminal + ECS 142 Lectures 7-8 57 Using Precedence to Solve S/R Conflicts тАв Same grammar as before E тЖТ E + E | E * E | int тАв We will also have the states [E тЖТ E + тАв E, +] [E тЖТ E + EтАв, +] [E тЖТ тАв E + E, +] тЗТE [E тЖТ E тАв + E, +] тАж тАж тАв Now we also have a shift/reduce on input + тАУ We choose reduce because E тЖТ E + E and + have the same precedence and + is left-associative ECS 142 Lectures 7-8 58 Using Precedence to Solve S/R Conflicts тАв Back to our dangling else example [S тЖТ

Title: PLY (Python Lex-Yacc) - David Beazley
URL: https://www.dabeaz.com/ply/ply.html
Content: A shift/reduce conflict is caused when the parser generator can't decide whether or not to reduce a rule or shift a symbol on the parsing stack.

Title: How to resolve parser conflicts - Learning - discuss OCaml
URL: https://discuss.ocaml.org/t/how-to-resolve-parser-conflicts/9314
Content: # How to resolve parser conflicts. `Warning: 9 states have shift/reduce conflicts. Warning: 2 states have reduce/reduce conflicts. Warning: 4 reduce/reduce conflicts were arbitrarily resolved.`. | BFUNC expr { FuncApp ($1, $2) }. | expr POW expr { ExpoApp ($1, $3) }. | expr MULT expr { Mult ($1, $3) }. | expr DIV expr { Mult ($1, ExpoApp ($3, Int (-1))) }. | expr PLUS expr { Add ($1, $3) }. | expr MINUS expr { Add ($1, Mult (Int (-1), $3)) }. | MINUS expr %prec SIGN { Mult (Int (-1), $2) }. | expr expr %prec PRODUCT { Mult ($1, $2) }. Your grammar has reduce-reduce conflicts stemming from the last rule `expr -> expr expr`. For example, `expr PLUS expr` can be reduced and produce either a тАЬ`Mult`тАЭ or an тАЬ`Add`тАЭ. I think what @nojb is trying to say, is that there is a basic understanding of how to write LR(1) (in the case of Menhir and Yacc) grammars, that you probably should gain.
